title: `podman inspect` does not return size of container terminal
body:
### Issue Description

In the output of `podman inspect`, there is a field for the container's TTY size (`ConsoleSize`), which Podman presently only returns [0,0] for. This should be fixed to return the actual terminal size for containers that attach a TTY.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman run -t -i --rm fedora bash`
2. `podman inspect -l`



### Describe the results you received

`ConsoleSize` returns [0,0]

### Describe the results you expected

A sensible value describing the actual size of the terminal

### podman info output

```yaml
N/A
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: I am a new contributor to podman, I would like to take this one :)

Hello @mheon, I was learning and going through the code base, from what knowledge I gained, 
I think we can introduce new field in `ContainerState` struct for height and width, which will hold the last resized size. 
The function that runs when SIGWINCH signal is generated and sends the current size, we can add a code for updating container state with new tty size, followed by calling save function for it to save in local db. 
Then in inspect config generate function we can use this values`(c.state.height & width)` to set values and return to user.
I would implement them practically, just wanted to ask if this approach seems right ?

I was thinking that using https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/term#GetSize function (which would require getting the container's PID, opening `/proc/$PID/fd/0`, and passing that to `GetSize`) - but your way is also perfectly acceptable.

Thanks @mheon for sharing your idea.
While implementing my method, I found that there are different pipelines for resizing `(attach/exec/api)` and if i use my method it would be lot of unrequired work by adding logic for each.
So, I started to implement with your way which is very simple and central implementation.
The only problem I got is,
to get the container PID either we can use `c.PID()` which
- checks lock
- syncs container
- returns `c.state.PID`

but this causes deadlock because [Inspect()](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/container_inspect.go#L39) already holds the lock, so `podman inspect`  just stucks.
So, I used `c.state.PID` directly which worked, but i think we need to sync the container before getting value of `state.PID`, in the generate function.
What are your thoughts on this?

Yeah, that's fine. `c.PID()` is meant for external callers, and as such it has to take the lock. Direct access to `c.state.PID` is safe as long as the lock is already held and the state has been updated from the database, both of which are already done by `Inspect()`
